feedback.text: I believe one of the biggest gaps in consumer protection online is the fact that consumers don't own the digital products that they buy, when these products have an online component. This applies to some software packages as well, but it's most prevalent when it comes to video-games. Game publishers sell games to customers, but make those games dependent on their servers in order to run. Some time later - which could be only a few months in some cases - the company takes the game's servers offline, and the game no longer runs, leaving the customer with a non-functional product. Of course, companies cannot be expected to keep servers running indefinitely; however, end-of-life plans should be put in place for when game servers are taken down, allowing players to keep using the products that they bought. This was the case for decades, including with multiplayer-only games, so it's not only technically feasible - it was the default, until relatively recently. I have signed the recent Stop Destroying Videogames ECI which has reached more than 1.4 million signatures so far, showing that there's great interest and support among EU citizens for this basic protection of consumer rights - the right to own our games.
